title: Tin roofs in Tondo
summary: A heat-island experiment in a Manila barangay painted tin roofs white. The temperatures fell. The friendships almost did too.
body:
Tondo, in north Manila, is one of the densest neighbourhoods in the Philippines. Most houses have tin roofs. In April, when the temperature outside reaches 36 degrees Celsius, the air just under the roof can reach 52. People sleep on the floor. They drink water through the night. Some climb to the roof at three in the morning to feel a breeze.

In 2021 a small NGO from the University of the Philippines arrived in one barangay with an experiment. They had read studies from India and Bangladesh which showed that painting tin roofs white, with cheap reflective paint, lowers indoor temperatures by between three and seven degrees. The materials cost about ninety pesos per roof. The NGO offered to paint, free, every roof in the barangay that wanted it. About sixty per cent of households agreed. The remaining forty per cent did not.

The results were measurable. In the painted houses, June afternoon temperatures fell on average by 4.8 degrees. Children slept better. A small clinic reported fewer heat-related visits. So far the story matches what the studies predicted.

What the studies had not predicted was the social texture. The painted roofs were visible from a long way off, especially from the higher floors of buildings on Roxas Boulevard. The barangay began to look, to outsiders, like a chessboard. Some residents felt the white roofs marked them as 'people who needed help'. Others felt the unpainted houses now looked dirtier than they had looked before, although they had not changed. A man whose roof was unpainted, by his own choice, said neighbours had stopped greeting him in the morning. A woman whose roof was painted said her sister-in-law, two streets away, had stopped visiting.

The NGO had to slow the programme. In the following year, they offered a second option: a slightly off-white paint, only marginally less reflective, that read from a distance as 'an ordinary tin roof that had aged in the sun.' Eighty per cent of holdout households accepted this option. Indoor temperatures fell by 4.1 degrees instead of 4.8. The social friction nearly disappeared.

When the NGO published its report, it did not present the off-white paint as a clever solution. It presented it as a lesson learned the hard way. The team wrote: 'We measured heat. We did not measure visibility. The temperature inside a house is not the only thing the house carries.'

The programme has now reached eleven barangays in Tondo. Three other Asian cities have adapted it. In each, the local team has been advised to begin with the slightly-off-white version, even if the bright white would cool the rooms a little more. The lost half-degree is treated as the cost of leaving people their dignity in the eyes of their neighbours.

annotations.meaningVi: tổ dân phố (đơn vị hành chính nhỏ ở Philippines)
kết cấu xã hội / quan hệ làng xóm
hộ giữ nguyên / không tham gia
ma sát xã hội / căng thẳng giữa hàng xóm
bài học rút ra khó nhọc
sự nhìn thấy / sự lộ ra ngoài
đã chỉnh sửa cho phù hợp
các lượt khám liên quan đến nóng
những ngôi nhà chưa được sơn

grammarNotes.title: Comparatives and adverbs of degree
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng so sánh và trạng từ chỉ mức độ để định lượng thay đổi. Trong bài: 'lowers indoor temperatures by between three and seven degrees' (định lượng cụ thể). 'fell on average by 4.8 degrees' (so sánh có số liệu). 'a slightly off-white paint, only marginally less reflective' ('marginally' = chỉ ít / không đáng kể). 'Eighty per cent of holdout households accepted' (so sánh phần trăm). 'a little more' (so sánh hơn rút gọn).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses comparatives and degree adverbs to quantify change. In the passage: 'lowers indoor temperatures by between three and seven degrees' (precise quantity). 'fell on average by 4.8 degrees' (numerical comparison). 'a slightly off-white paint, only marginally less reflective' ('marginally' = only a little). 'Eighty per cent of holdout households accepted' (percentage comparison). 'a little more' (short comparative).

translationVi:
Tondo, ở bắc Manila, là một trong những khu đông nhất Philippines. Đa số nhà có mái tôn. Tháng Tư, khi ngoài trời 36 độ C, không khí ngay dưới mái có thể tới 52. Người ta ngủ trên sàn. Uống nước suốt đêm. Có người trèo lên mái lúc ba giờ sáng để hưởng chút gió.

Năm 2021 một tổ chức phi chính phủ nhỏ thuộc Đại học Philippines đến một barangay với một thí nghiệm. Họ đọc nghiên cứu từ Ấn Độ và Bangladesh cho thấy sơn trắng mái tôn bằng sơn phản xạ rẻ tiền hạ nhiệt độ trong nhà từ ba đến bảy độ. Vật liệu khoảng chín mươi peso mỗi mái. Tổ chức đề nghị sơn miễn phí mọi mái nhà trong barangay nếu muốn. Khoảng sáu mươi phần trăm hộ đồng ý. Bốn mươi phần trăm còn lại thì không.

Kết quả đo được. Ở những nhà được sơn, nhiệt độ chiều tháng Sáu giảm trung bình 4.8 độ. Trẻ con ngủ ngon hơn. Một phòng khám nhỏ ghi nhận ít ca liên quan đến nóng hơn. Đến đây câu chuyện khớp với nghiên cứu.

Điều nghiên cứu chưa đoán trước là 'kết cấu xã hội'. Mái sơn trắng nhìn thấy từ xa, đặc biệt từ các tầng cao của các toà nhà trên đại lộ Roxas. Barangay bắt đầu giống một bàn cờ với người ngoài. Một số dân cảm thấy mái trắng đánh dấu họ là 'người cần giúp đỡ'. Số khác cảm thấy nhà chưa sơn giờ trông bẩn hơn trước, dù chúng không thay đổi. Một người đàn ông có mái chưa sơn, do tự ông chọn, nói hàng xóm thôi chào ông buổi sáng. Một phụ nữ có mái đã sơn nói chị dâu cách hai phố đã ngừng đến thăm.

Tổ chức phải làm chậm chương trình. Năm sau, họ đưa ra lựa chọn thứ hai: một loại sơn hơi-trắng, phản xạ kém hơn một chút thôi, từ xa nhìn như 'mái tôn thường đã bạc dưới nắng'. Tám mươi phần trăm hộ trước đó không tham gia đã chấp nhận. Nhiệt độ trong nhà giảm 4.1 độ thay vì 4.8. Ma sát xã hội gần như biến mất.

Khi tổ chức công bố báo cáo, họ không trình bày sơn hơi-trắng như một giải pháp khéo léo. Họ trình bày nó như một bài học rút ra một cách khó nhọc. Nhóm viết: 'Chúng tôi đo nhiệt. Chúng tôi không đo sự hiện diện trong mắt người khác. Nhiệt độ trong nhà không phải thứ duy nhất ngôi nhà mang theo.'

Chương trình nay đã tới mười một barangay ở Tondo. Ba thành phố châu Á khác đã chỉnh sửa để dùng. Ở mỗi nơi, đội địa phương được khuyên bắt đầu từ phiên bản hơi-trắng, dù trắng sáng sẽ làm phòng mát hơn chút. Nửa độ mất đi được coi như cái giá của việc giữ cho người ta phẩm giá trong mắt hàng xóm.

questions.prompt: How hot can the air under a Tondo tin roof get in April?
What did the NGO offer in 2021?
options: Free reflective white paint for any roof in the barangay; materials about 90 pesos each

questions.prompt: How many households accepted, and how many refused?
What measurable result did they see?
options: June afternoon temperatures fell by an average of 4.8°C in painted houses; fewer heat-clinic visits

questions.prompt: What had the studies NOT predicted?
options: The social texture — friction, visibility, neighbours' judgement

questions.prompt: Why did some residents resent the painted roofs?
options: The white roofs visibly marked them as 'people who needed help' from far away (e.g. Roxas Boulevard towers)

questions.prompt: What social consequence happened to the unpainted-roof man?
options: Neighbours stopped greeting him in the morning

questions.prompt: How did the NGO adjust in the second year?
options: Offered a slightly off-white paint, marginally less reflective, that looked like a sun-aged roof from a distance

questions.prompt: How well did the off-white version work?
options: 80% of holdout households accepted; cooling of 4.1°C instead of 4.8°C; friction almost gone

questions.prompt: How did the team describe their lesson in the report?
options: 'We measured heat. We did not measure visibility.' — the house carries more than its temperature

questions.prompt: What advice is given to cities adopting the programme?
options: Start with off-white even though bright white would cool rooms a little more — the lost half-degree is the cost of dignity
Skip Tondo's mistakes by paying residents
A technical fix that works on paper can damage the social fabric of a neighbourhood — and a slightly worse paint that preserves dignity may be the better solution.
Tondo is too hot to live in.

In April, when the temperature outside reaches 36 degrees Celsius, the air just under the roof can reach 52. People sleep on the floor. They drink water through the night. Some climb to the roof at three in the morning to feel a breeze.

They had read studies from India and Bangladesh which showed that painting tin roofs white, with cheap {{b3}} paint, lowers indoor temperatures by between three and seven degrees. The remaining forty per cent did not.

The results were measurable. In the painted houses, June afternoon temperatures fell on average by {{b5}} degrees. Children slept better. A small clinic reported fewer heat-related visits. So far the story matches what the studies predicted.

What the studies had not predicted was the social {{b6}}. The painted roofs were visible from a long way off, especially from the higher floors of buildings on Roxas Boulevard. The barangay began to look, to outsiders, like a chessboard. Some residents felt the white roofs marked them as 'people who needed {{b7}}'. Others felt the unpainted houses now looked dirtier than they had looked before, although they had not changed. A man whose roof was unpainted, by his own choice, said neighbours had stopped greeting him in the morning. A woman whose roof was painted said her sister-in-law, two streets away, had stopped {{b8}}.

The NGO had to slow the programme. In the following year, they offered a second option: a slightly {{b9}}-white paint, only marginally less reflective, that read from a distance as 'an ordinary tin roof that had aged in the sun.' Eighty per cent of holdout households accepted this option. Indoor temperatures fell by 4.1 degrees instead of 4.8. The social friction nearly disappeared.

When the NGO published its report, it did not present the off-white paint as a clever solution. It presented it as a lesson learned the hard way. The team wrote: 'We measured heat. We did not measure {{b10}}. The temperature inside a house is not the only thing the house carries.'

The programme has now reached eleven barangays in Tondo. Three other Asian cities have adapted it. In each, the local team has been advised to begin with the slightly-off-white version, even if the bright white would cool the rooms a little more. The lost half-degree is treated as the cost of leaving people their {{b11}} in the eyes of their neighbours.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The team said: 'The temperature inside a house is not the only thing the house carries.' Think of an improvement someone offered you or your family for free (a service, a renovation, a free clinic visit). What else did the offer 'carry' — visibility, judgement, a label — that you had to weigh against the benefit?
